planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile potting mix or garden soil in partial shade to bright indirect light after all frost danger has passed; New Guinea impatiens requires more sun than standard impatiens to bloom well but still demands protection from hot afternoon sun.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile potting mix in partial shade to bright indirect light after frost; New Guinea impatiens is a hybrid group derived from Pacific island species, producing much larger flowers (2–3 inches diameter) than common impatiens (I. walleriana) in an even broader color range — vivid red, orange, hot pink, salmon, lavender, white, and bicolors — on compact, bushy plants with attractive, often dark-bronze or deep-green, glossy foliage. They are significantly more sun-tolerant and heat-resistant than common impatiens (though they still need protection from hot afternoon sun) and are resistant to the downy mildew disease that devastated common impatiens in many regions after 2011. Self-cleaning (no deadheading required). Excellent in combination planters, window boxes, and hanging baskets in dappled light. In zones 10–12 the plants persist as tender perennials; in cooler climates they are treated as frost-tender annuals.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost; cup flower performs well through summer heat and is reliably self-cleaning, requiring no deadheading for continuous bloom.

directions: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost or plant container specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; cup flower is a South American perennial grown as an annual that produces a continuous carpet of small, upward-facing, violet-blue to lavender-purple or white cup-shaped flowers with a distinctive yellow eye from spring through frost — one of the few reliably violet-blue annuals that performs well in both containers and the garden border through summer heat. Plants are neat, mounding, and self-cleaning (no deadheading required) and are excellent combined with yellow marigolds, orange zinnias, and white petunias for high-contrast container plantings. In zones 7–9 it persists as a short-lived perennial, overwintering and blooming heavily in late winter and spring. 'Purple Robe', 'Mont Blanc' (white), and 'Violet Waterfall' are popular container selections. Excellent for edging, rock gardens, and container edges.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root stem-tip cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun a few weeks before last frost; twinspur performs best in cool weather and may go semi-dormant in peak summer heat, rebounding in fall.

directions: Root stem-tip cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun in cool spring or fall weather; twinspur is a South African relative of snapdragons that produces delicate, open-faced, two-lipped flowers of soft to vivid pink, salmon, coral, or white with characteristic twin spurs projecting backward from the base of each flower — a charming, cottage-garden character of the individual flowers. Plants produce a loose, mounding, spreading habit with abundant bloom in cool spring and fall weather and are self-cleaning (no deadheading required). Like bacopa and lobelia, they may go semi-dormant in peak summer heat and should be trimmed back slightly and watered through the worst heat to encourage fall rebloom. Excellent in hanging baskets, window boxes, and container edges. 'Coral Belle', 'Elegance Series', and 'Whisper Series' are widely grown. Perennial in zones 7–9 where winters remain above 25°F.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and set out on a trellis, fence, or lattice in partial shade to full sun after frost; climbing snapdragon climbs by twisting its leaf stalks (petioles) around thin supports and is one of the few climbing annuals that flowers well in partial shade.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and set out on a trellis, lattice, or fence in partial shade after frost; climbing snapdragon is a Mexican native annual vine that climbs by twisting its leaf stalks around thin supports, covering itself with snapdragon-like, tubular, two-lipped flowers of rosy purple, lavender, pink, or white from summer until frost. The triangular, softly hairy, ivy-shaped leaves and twining, scandent growth create a soft, romantic effect on trellises, fences, and mailbox posts. It is notably more shade-tolerant than most annual vines and is one of the few that blooms freely in filtered shade — valuable for shaded pergolas, north-facing fences, and areas receiving only 4–5 hours of direct sun. Hummingbirds are attracted to the tubular flowers. 'Bride's White', 'Mystic Pink', and 'Violet Purple' are the most widely available color selections. In zones 9–11, the plant may overwinter as a short-lived perennial.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade after frost; tweedia is one of the very few true pure sky-blue flowers available as an annual, making it exceptionally valuable as a cut flower and container accent.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost or plant container specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; tweedia (blue milkweed) is a Brazilian native sub-shrub grown as an annual that produces one of the rarest colors in the flower world — a pure, clear, true sky-blue that does not shade toward purple or lavender. The small, star-shaped flowers open turquoise-blue from pink buds, gradually deepen to rich sky-blue at maturity, and then age through lilac to pink as they fade, providing three distinct flower colors simultaneously on the same plant. The soft, hairy, heart-shaped leaves and lax, scrambling stems give the plant a relaxed, cottage-garden character. Excellent as a cut flower — the unusual blue color is in extreme demand for floral arrangements and wedding work. Plants bloom most freely in full sun but tolerate partial shade. In zones 9–11 it overwinters as a perennial sub-shrub.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
